63
Table of Contents

 

Revenues

 

Our revenue decreased by $4,397,091, or 31.9%, from $13,797,661 for the six months ended March 31, 2022 to $9,400,570 for the six months ended March 31, 2023, primarily due to the decrease in the air freight forwarding services in 2023. Our revenue from integrated cross-border logistics services decreased by $852,285, or 8.72%, from $9,775,509 for the six months ended March 31, 2022 to $8,923,224 for the six months ended March 31, 2023. The revenue from integrated cross-border logistics services decreased since the market supply started recovering from the pandemic of Covid-19, leading to increased market competition.

 

The following table set forth the breakdown of our revenue analysis for integrated cross-border logistics services for the periods indicated:

 

   Six months ended March 31, 
   2022   2023 
Average daily number of packages   6,041    5,790 
Average daily freight weight (kilogram)   2,733    2,396 
Average daily number of shipments   2.76    3.32 
Average daily revenue per freight weight  $27.74   $21.56 

 

Our revenue from air freight forwarding services decreased by $3,544,806, or 88.1%, from $4,022,152 for the six months ended March 31, 2022 to $477,346 for the six months ended March 31, 2023. The Company sells cargo spaces obtained under the block space agreements to other air freight forwarders by leveraging the price differences to earn revenue. Such revenue is recognized upon the completion of the transaction as air freight forwarding service revenue. For the six months ended March 31, 2022, the overall air freight forwarding industry has been materially affected due to the pandemic, which resulted in the tight market supplies. However, as we have managed to maintain our logistics and freight services in stable with the block space agreement entered with airline, leading to more sales orders and service revenue for the six months ended March 31, 2022.

 

The decrease of the air freight forwarding services for the six months ended March 31, 2023 is primarily due to the decrease of revenue derived from such resale of cargo space, as the overall supply of the air freight cargo spaces in the market has been increased due to the gradual resumption of air freight capacity after the pandemic, leading to the increased flight frequency and additional air freight routes, and eventually to a decrease in airfare prices compared to the time during the pandemic. As a result, the Company has less incentive to sell air freight spaces to other freight forwarders.

 

During the six months ended March 31, 2022 and 2023, majority of our revenue transactions were transacted in Hong Kong Dollars, with the remaining revenue transactions transacted in United States Dollars and we had not recorded any foreign exchange fluctuations impacts on our revenue.

 

Cost of Revenue

 

The following table set forth the breakdown of our cost of revenue for the periods indicated:

 

   Six months ended March 31, 
   2022   2023 
Air freight charges  $7,714,565   $3,565,445 
Last mile carriage and alliance costs   5,310,022    5,053,500 
Warehouse labor costs   73,878    51,943 
Packing costs   21,728    13,909 
   $13,120,193   $8,684,797 

 

Our cost of revenue mainly represented air freight charges, last mile carriage and alliance costs, packaging costs and labor costs. Our cost of revenue decreased by $4,435,396, or 33.8%, from $13,120,193 for the six months ended March 31, 2022 to $8,684,797 for the six months ended March 31, 2023, mainly due to decrease in air freight and courier expenses which in line with the drop of the revenue from air freight forwarding services.

 

Our air freight charges mainly represented costs of air freight services. Our air freight charges decreased by $4,149,120, or 53.8%, from $7,714,565 for the six months ended March 31, 2022 to $3,565,445 for the six months ended March 31, 2023, mainly due to the decreased sales from both air freight forwarding services and integrated cross-border logistics services during the six months ended March 31, 2023.

 

Our last mile carriage and alliance costs mainly represented courier service charges, customs clearance fees and other alliance service charges. Our last mile carriage and alliance costs decreased by $256,522, or 4.8%, from $5,310,022 for the six months ended March 31, 2022 to $5,053,500 for the six months ended March 31, 2023, last mile carriage and alliance costs remain stable in both periods.

 

Our warehouse labor costs mainly represented salaries and wages of warehouse staff. Our warehouse labor costs decreased by $21,935, or 29.7%, from $73,878 for the six months ended March 31, 2022 to $51,943 for the six months ended March 31, 2023, mainly due to less part-time workers hired.

 

Our packing costs mainly represented packing materials, including boxes and labels, for repacking customers’ products. Our packing costs decreased by $7,819, or 36.0%, from $21,728 for the six months ended March 31, 2022 to $13,909 for the six months ended March 31, 2023, mainly due to the drop of the costs of packing materials.

 

Gross Profit

 

Our gross profit increased by $38,305, or 5.7%, from $677,468 for the six months ended March 31, 2022 to $715,773 for the six months ended March 31, 2023. Our gross profit margin increased to 7.6% for the six months ended March 31, 2023, from 4.9% for the six months ended March 31, 2022. The increase in gross profit margin could be attributed to our costs controlled effectively by liaising better terms and lower airfare charges with suppliers under the gradual resumption of air freight capacity after the pandemic.

General and Administrative Expenses

   $303,574   $272,113 

 

Our general and administrative expenses mainly represented staff costs, traveling expenses, depreciation charge, amortization of right-of-use assets, allowance for expected credit loss and other administrative expenses. Our general and administrative expenses decreased by $31,461, or 10.4%, from $303,574 for the six months ended March 31, 2022 to $272,113 for the six months ended March 31, 2023, mainly due to decrease in staff costs, motor vehicle expenses and amortization of right-of-use assets as the Company early terminated an operating lease and had no motor vehicle during the six months ended March 31, 2023.

 

Our staff costs mainly represent staff salaries, contribution to staff retirement benefits and staff welfare for office staff and director. Staff costs decreased by $24,219, or 12.0%, from $201,429 for the six months ended March 31, 2022 to $177,210 for the six months ended March 31, 2023. This was mainly due to the decrease in headcount during the six months ended March 31, 2023.

 

Our travel expenses decreased by $8,923, or 26.1%, from $34,238 for the six months ended March 31, 2022 to $25,315 for the six months ended March 31, 2023. The travelling expenses remain stables for both periods.

 

Our amortization of right-of-use assets mainly represented our operating lease of our Hong Kong office and warehouse on 18th and 24th floors of Tsuen Wan Industrial Centre. Our depreciation charge mainly represented depreciation of our fixture, furniture and office equipment. Our amortization of right-of-use assets and depreciation charge decreased for the six months ended March 31, 2023 mainly due to the lease of the 24th floor of Tsuen Wan Industrial Centre has been expired during the six months ended March 31, 2023.

 

Other Income/Expenses

 

Our other income mainly consists of interest income, interest expenses, foreign exchange gain/loss, government grants and insurance claim income. Our net other income was $63,409 for the six months ended March 31, 2022, as compared to net other income of $73,346 for the six months ended March 31, 2023, primarily due to increase in foreign exchange gain net off by the decrease in the reimbursement of the insurance claim.

 

The foreign exchange gains of $32,644 and $71,299 for the six months ended March 31, 2022 and 2023, respectively, primarily as a result of net variances of the exchange rate between the Australian dollars and Hong Kong dollars on Australian dollar-denominated transactions. The foreign exchange gains were entirely derived from our foreign exchange impact derived from our costs of revenues only and we have not recorded any impact of foreign exchange fluctuations on our revenue.

 

During the six months ended March 31, 2022 and 2023, the foreign currency fluctuations on the Company are not hedged by any currency borrowings or other hedging instruments.

 

Income Tax Expense

 

The Company is subject to income taxes on an entity basis on income arising in or derived from the tax jurisdiction in which each entity is domiciled.

 

Cayman Islands and British Virgin Islands (“BVI”)

 

The Company is incorporated in the Cayman Islands and its wholly-owned subsidiary is incorporated in BVI. Under the current laws of the Cayman Islands and the BVI, these entities are not subject to income or capital gains taxes. In addition, dividend payments are not subject to withholdings tax in the Cayman Islands and the BVI.

 

Hong Kong

 

The Company generated substantially all of its taxable income in the Hong Kong for the six months ended March 31, 2022 and 2023. Accordingly, tax expenses records in the Company’s result of operations are almost entirely attributable to income earned in the Hong Kong.

 

The Hong Kong profits tax is calculated at 8.25% on the first HK$2 million of the estimated assessable profits and at 16.5% on the estimated assessable profits above HK$2 million.

 

The effective tax rates on income before income taxes for the six months ended March 31, 2022 and 2023 were approximately 11.3% and 12.8%, respectively.

 

Australia

 

Australian companies are subject to a corporate income tax rate of 30% on their taxable income, other than those classified as a “base rate company”, which are businesses with revenue of less than A$50 million (US$78 million) that are subject to a reduced corporate income tax rate of 25%. For the six months ended March 31, 2022 and 2023, the Company was not considered a taxable Australian company.

 

New Zealand

 

New Zealand companies are subject to a corporate income tax rate of 28% on their taxable income. For the six months ended March 31, 2022 and 2023, the Company was not considered a taxable New Zealand company.

 

65
Table of Contents

 

Net Income

 

Our net income increased by $62,951 or 16.2% to $450,808 for the six months ended March 31, 2023, as compared to $387,857 for six months ended March 31, 2022. The increase in net income was predominantly due to increased gross profit from the integrated cross-border logistics services.

 

Liquidity and Capital Resources

 

For the six months ended March 31, 2022 and 2023 and year ended September 30, 2021 and 2022, we have financed our operations primarily through cash generated from our business operation and capital contributions by our shareholder.

 

As of September 30, 2021, we had working capital of $1,349,829 as compared to working capital of $499,500 as of September 30, 2022. The total current assets decreased 14.1%, from $2,463,405 on September 30, 2021 to $2,116,028 in September 30, 2022, mainly because of a decrease in cash and cash equivalents. The total current liabilities increased 45.2%, from $1,113,576 on September 30, 2021 to $1,616,528 as of September 30, 2022. The increase in our current liabilities is mainly due to an increase in accounts payables.

 

As of March 31, 2023, we have working capital of $999,716 as compared to working capital of $499,500 as of September 30, 2022. The total current assets increased 47.7%, from $2,116,028 on September 30, 2022 to $3,125,983 in March 31, 2023, mainly because of an increase in cash and cash equivalents as a result of the profit attributable to the Company for the six months ended March 31, 2023. The total current liabilities increased 31.5%, from $1,616,528 on September 30, 2022 to $2,126,267 as of March 31, 2023. The increase in our current liabilities is mainly due to an increase in accounts payables.

 

We also received capital injections by our shareholder of nil, $126,923 and Nil for the years ended September 30, 2021 and 2022 and for the six months ended March 31, 2023, respectively.

 

While our accounts receivable increased from $825,836 as of September 30, 2021 to $1,111,998 as of September 30, 2022, our working capital deceased from $1,349,829 as of September 30, 2021 to $499,500 as of September 30, 2022. The decrease of working capital was mainly due to a dividend paid of $1,244,502 for the year ended September 30, 2022.

 

Based on our total cash and cash equivalents as of March 31, 2023, the cash inflows from operating activities, we did not experience or identify any material trends or any known demands, commitments, events or uncertainties, in our liquidity, capital resources and results of operations, such as material commitments for capital expenditures and deposit on a short-term basis. We believe that our current cash and cash equivalents and cash flows provided by operating activities will be sufficient to meet our working capital needs in the next 12 months following this offering.

 

In long run, if we need additional capital in the future to fund our continued operations and that our cash requirements exceed the amount of cash and cash equivalents we have on hand at that time, we may seek to issue equity or debt securities or obtain credit facilities. The issuance and sale of additional equity or convertible loans would result in dilution to our shareholders. The occurrence of indebtedness would result in increased fixed obligations and could result in operating covenants that might restrict our operations. We cannot assure you that financing will be available in amounts or on terms acceptable to us, if at all.

Cash and cash equivalents

 

Cash and cash equivalents consist of funds deposited with banks, which are highly liquid and are unrestricted as to withdrawal or use.

 

The total balance of cash and cash equivalents decreased from $1.0 million as of September 30, 2021 to $0.6 million as of September 30, 2022. The decrease in the balance of cash and cash equivalents was mainly due to the net cash generated from operation of $783,045 which offset with the dividend paid of $1,244,502 for the year ended September 30, 2022.

 

The total balance of cash and cash equivalents increased from $0.6 million as of September 30, 2022 to $1.6 million as of March 31, 2023. The increase in the balance of cash and cash equivalents was mainly due to the net cash generated from operation of $1,157,621 which offset with the cash payment for the offering expenses of $110,000 for the six months ended March 31, 2023.

 

Accounts Receivable, net

 

Our accounts receivable represented receivables from customers of our logistics and air freight forwarding services. Credit periods for customers are normally within 30 days after customers have received the services provided by the Company.

 

Our accounts receivable, net increased by $286,162, or 34.7% from $825,836 as of September 30, 2021 to $1,111,998 as of September 30, 2022. The increase was mainly attributable to the increase in revenue near the year end.

 

Our accounts receivable, net decreased by $293,684, or 26.4% from $1,111,998 as of September 30, 2022 to $818,314 as of March 31, 2023. The decrease was mainly attributable to the decrease in revenue near the period end.

 

An impairment analysis is performed at the end of each year. For the year ended September 30, 2021, there was a reversal of allowance for expected credit loss amounting to $27,054 and a reversal of allowance for expected credit loss for $522 was made of the year ended September 30, 2022. There was an allowance for expected credit loss amounting to $12,072 made in the six months ended March 31, 2023

 

Amount due from a director

 

Amount due from a director decreased from $226,484 as of September 30, 2021 to nil as of September 30, 2022. This line item represents the fund advance to the director by the subsidiary of the Company, which are non-trade, unsecured, non-interest bearing and repayable on demand. The balance was substantially offset with dividend declaration.

 

Deposits and prepayment

 

Deposits and prepayment consist of trade deposits, deposits of rental and utility and prepayment paid to suppliers.

 

Deposits and prepayment decreased by $7,404, or 39.1% from $18,942 as of September 30, 2021 to $11,538 as of September 30, 2022. The decrease was mainly due to the decrease in prepayment with suppliers.

 

Deposits and prepayment increased by $64,424, or 558.4% from $11,538 as of September 30, 2022 to $75,962 as of March 31, 2023. The increase was mainly due to the increase in prepayment with suppliers.

 

Contract assets

 

Contract assets represent estimated amounts for which the Company has the right to consideration for the services provided while a delivery is still in-transit but for which it has not yet completed the performance obligation and has not yet invoiced the customer. Upon completion of the performance obligations, which can vary in duration based upon the method of transport and billing the customer, these amounts become classified within accounts receivable. Contract assets increased by $71,053 or 19.5% from $363,704 as of September 30, 2021 to $434,757 as of September 30, 2022 and further increased by $84,418 or 19.4% to $519,175 as of March 31, 2023. The increase was mainly due to more in-transit deliveries that has not yet invoiced the customers near the year end 2022 and the six months ended March 31, 2023.

 

67
Table of Contents

 

Deferred costs

 

Deferred offering costs consist principally of all direct offering costs incurred by the Company, such as underwriting, legal, accounting, consulting, printing, and other registration related costs in connection with the Initial Public Offering (“IPO”) of the Company’s ordinary shares. Such costs are deferred until the closing of the offering, at which time the deferred costs are offset against the offering proceeds. In the event the offering is unsuccessful or aborted, the costs will be expensed.

 

Accounts payable

 

The accounts payable are derived from logistics and air freight service providers. The accounts payable increased by $344,664, or 35.7% from $966,403 as of September 30, 2021 to $1,311,067 as of September 30, 2022. The balances arise from logistics service providers were settled within 7 to 30 days. The increase was mainly due to outstanding supplier invoices of $534,804 related to the air freight costs for the year ended September 30, 2022, which were issued subsequently after the year end date and resulted in an increase in the outstanding balance as of September 30, 2022.

 

The accounts payable increased by $412,926 or 31.5% from $1,311,067 as of September 30, 2022 to $1,723,993 as of March 31, 2023. The increase was mainly due to outstanding supplier payment of $1,587,372 related to the air freight costs for the period ended March 31, 2023, which were paid subsequent to March 31, 2023.

 

Accounts payable – related party

 

Accounts payable – related party amounted to $175,479 and $309,083 as of September 30, 2022 and March 31, 2023, respectively. This balance consists of accounts payable to a related company arising from unsettled courier service fees. As the subsidiary of the Company started to have business with that related party during the year ended September 30, 2022, there was no such balance as of September 30, 2021.

 

The accounts payable – related party increased by $133,604 or 76.1 % from $175,479 as of September 30, 2022 to $309,083 as of March 31, 2023. The increase was mainly due to increase in outstanding supplier invoices related to unsettled courier service fees for the period ended March 31, 2023

 

Other payables and accrued liabilities

 

The line item consists of accrued payroll expenses, audit fees and other administrative expenses. The balance decreased by $5,415, or 29.3% from $18,458 as of September 30, 2021 to $13,043 as of September 30, 2022, the decrease was mainly due to the decrease of accrued administrative expenses.

 

The balance increased by $34,538, or 264.8% from $13,043 as of September 30, 2022 to $47,581 as of March 31, 2023, the increase was mainly due to the increase of accrued payroll expenses.

 

Contract liabilities

 

Contract liabilities are recognized when the Company receives prepayments from customers resulting from in-transit logistics. Contract liabilities will be recognized as revenue when promised services are provided. The balance decreased by $24,157 from $24,157 as of September 30, 2021 to nil as of September 30, 2022, the decrease was mainly due to the decrease in prepayment received from customers for in-transit logistics transactions near the year ends.

 

Lease liabilities – current

 

Our lease liabilities represented the current portion of the operating lease of our Hong Kong office and warehouse.

 

Effective October 1, 2020, the Company adopted the new lease accounting standard using a modified retrospective transition method, which allows the Company not to recast comparative periods presented in its consolidated financial statements. In addition, the Company elected the package of practical expedients, which allows the Company to not reassess whether any existing contracts contain a lease, to not reassess historical lease classification as operating or finance leases, and to not reassess initial direct costs. The Company combines the lease and non-lease components in determining the ROU assets and related lease obligation. Adoption of this standard resulted in the recording of operating lease ROU assets and corresponding operating lease liabilities. ROU assets and related lease obligations are recognized at commencement date based on the present value of remaining lease payments over the lease term.

 

The Company’s management believes that the Hong Kong Dollar Best Lending Rate (“BLR”) was the most indicative rate of the Company’s borrowing cost for the calculation of the present value of the lease payments; the rate used by the Company as quoted by the BLR minus 2.5%

Our use of cash is primarily related to operating activities, payment of dividends and payment of deferred IPO cost. We have historically financed our operations primarily through our cash flow generated from our operations. Operating Activities

 

Our cash inflow from operating activities was principally from the receipt of revenue. Our cash outflow used in operating activities was principally for payment of supplier costs and operating expenses.

 

Net cash provided by operating activities was $783,045 for the year ended September 30, 2022, compared to net cash provided by operating activities of $875,086 for the year ended September 30, 2021, representing a decrease of approximately $92,000 in the net cash inflow in operating activities. The decrease in net cash provided by operating activities was primarily due to the following major working capital changes:

 

(1) Change in accounts receivable resulted in a cash outflow of $285,640 for the year ended September 30, 2022 compared to a cash outflow of $40,500 for the same period of 2021, which led to an approximately $245,000 decrease in net cash inflow in operating activities.
(2) Change in deposits and prepayment resulted in a cash outflow of $146,442 for the year ended September 30, 2022 compared to a cash outflow of $26,883 for the same period of 2021, which led to an approximately $120,000 decrease in net cash inflow in operating activities.
(3) Change in accounts payable and accounts payable – related party resulted in a cash inflow of $520,143 for the year ended September 30, 2022 compared to a cash inflow of $382,881 for the same period of 2021, which led to an approximately $137,000 increase in net cash inflow in operating activities.
(4) Change in contract liabilities resulted in a cash outflow of $24,157 for the year ended September 30, 2022 compared to a cash inflow of $24,157 for the same period of 2021, which led to an approximately $48,000 decrease in net cash inflow in operating activities.
(5) Change in tax payables resulted in a cash outflow of $13,975 for the year ended September 30, 2022 compared to a cash inflow of $55,954 for the same period of 2021, which led to an approximately $70,000 decrease in net cash inflow in operating activities.
(6) Net income of $810,227 in the year ended September 30, 2022 compared net income of $564,758 to the same period of 2021, which led to an approximately $245,000 increase in net cash inflow in operating activities.

 

Net cash provided by operating activities was $1,157,621 for the six months ended March 31, 2023, compared to net cash provided by operating activities of $844,910 for the six months ended March 31, 2022, representing an increase of approximately $313,000 in the net cash inflow in operating activities. The increase in net cash provided by operating activities was primarily due to the following major working capital changes:

 

(1) Change in accounts receivable resulted in a cash inflow of $281,612 for the six months ended March 31, 2023 compared to a cash inflow of $161,770 for the same period of 2022, which led to an approximately $120,000 increase in net cash inflow in operating activities.
(2) Change in deposits and prepayment resulted in a cash outflow of $64,424 for the six months ended March 31, 2023 compared to a cash outflow of $112,981 for the same period of 2022, which led to an approximately $49,000 increase in net cash inflow in operating activities.
(3) Change in contract assets resulted in a cash outflow of $94,320 for the six months ended March 31, 2023 compared to a cash outflow of $252,838 for the same period of 2022, which led to an approximately $159,000 increase in net cash inflow in operating activities.
(4) Change in accounts payable resulted in a cash inflow of $412,926 for the six months ended March 31, 2023 compared to a cash outflow of $62,457 for the same period of 2022, which led to an approximately $475,000 increase in net cash inflow in operating activities.
(5) Change in accounts payable – related parties resulted in a cash inflow of $133,604 for the six months ended March 31, 2023 compared to a cash inflow of $826,569 for the same period of 2022, which led to an approximately $693,000 decrease in net cash inflow in operating activities.
(6) Change in tax payables resulted in a cash outflow of $23,157 for the six months ended March 31, 2023 compared to a cash outflow of $91,090 for the same period of 2022, which led to an approximately $68,000 increase in net cash inflow in operating activities.
(7) Net income of $450,808 in the six months ended March 31, 2023 compared net income of $387,857 to the same period of 2022, which led to an approximately $63,000 increase in net cash inflow in operating activities.

 

Investing Activities

 

For the years ended September 30, 2021, 2022 and for the six months ended March 31, 2023, our cash outflow used in investing activities was principally derived from the purchases of fixtures, furniture and equipment.

 

Financing Activities

 

For the year ended September 30, 2021, our cash used in financing activities was principally for advance to a related party. For the year ended September 30, 2022, our cash used in financing activities was principally for cash payment of dividends. For the six months ended March 31, 2023 our cash used in financing activities was principally for cash payment for the offering expenses

 

The Company believes that, taking into consideration the financial resources presently available, including the current levels of cash and cash flows from operations, our cash and cash equivalent will be sufficient to meet its anticipated cash needs for at least the next twelve months from the date of this report.

 

69
Table of Contents

 

Capital Expenditures

 

The Company did not incur any significant capital expenditure for the years ended September 30, 2021 and 2022 and for the six months ended March 31, 2023.

 

Off-Balance Sheet Arrangements

 

The Company is required to provide bank guarantee to IATA in favor of its associated airlines to secure the purchases of cargo spaces. IATA shall have the right from time to time by giving notice in writing to require us to increase the amount of guarantee if the cargo spaces purchased by us were greater than the existing guaranteed sum. Bank guarantees are provided by the principal bank of the Company, which in return required personal guarantee from a director of the Company and collateral such as mortgage over a property of the director to be pledged in favor of the bank. As of September 30, 2021, 2022 and March 31, 2023, a bank provided guarantee of $5,128, $232,051 and $232,051, respectively, for covering the performance of obligations of the Company.

 

Save as disclosed above, we have no off-balance sheet arrangements, including arrangements that would affect its liquidity, capital resources, market risk support, credit risk support, or other benefits.
